headline: FEEDERS TUMBLE, GRAINS DRIFT SIDEWAYS
subheadline: Cattle complex breaks as screwworm quarantine hits South Texas.
lead: Feeder cattle led the complex lower, down 1.8% to $342.57 as USDA confirmed the first New World screwworm case in decades, triggering quarantines in South Texas. Grains marked time in narrow ranges with corn up a penny at $4.26 and beans off 3 cents at $11.47, fund liquidation slowing but demand still sluggish. The screwworm news shifts cattle psychology from seasonal strength to biological risk, while grain's sideways drift suggests the liquidation question might be answering itself through exhaustion rather than weather catalysts.
the_takeaway: Screwworm shifts cattle from seasonal to biological risk, grains drifting toward resolution.
teaser: Cattle breaks on screwworm news while grains grind toward liquidation exhaustion.

one_number.unit: miles from US border where screwworm was first detected
one_number.context: The parasitic fly was found in Mexico just 31 miles from the US border in November 2024, making today's confirmed case in South Texas the feared progression cattle markets had been pricing since then. The proximity explains why USDA moved so fast on quarantines and why feeder cattle dropped harder than live cattle.

yesterdays_call.summary: Fund liquidation deepening, China talk not enough to reverse the slide.
yesterdays_call.note: Grains continued grinding lower on sluggish demand, confirming the liquidation thesis held control.

sections.title: CATTLE BREAKS ON SCREWWORM
sections.body: Feeder cattle fell to $342.57, down 1.8% as **USDA confirmed the first New World screwworm case in the US since the 1980s**. A 3-week-old calf in La Pryor, Texas tested positive for the parasitic fly that eats warm-blooded animals alive. Live cattle held better at $237.85, down 0.8%, but the damage is psychological more than immediate. Screwworm was detected 31 miles from the US border in Mexico last November, so this progression was always the fear scenario. USDA moved fast with quarantines and movement restrictions, but **the cattle complex just shifted from pricing seasonal strength to biological containment risk**.
sections.bottom_line: Biological risk replaces seasonal strength as the cattle story.

sections.title: GRAINS DRIFT THROUGH RANGE
sections.body: Corn ticked up a penny to $4.26 while beans eased 3 cents to $11.47, both moving inside yesterday's ranges on **sluggish demand and slowing fund liquidation**. December corn held $4.54, suggesting the selling pressure that drove the complex lower all week is finally running out of steam. China's renewed soybean purchase commitments still aren't translating to sustained futures support, but the technical selling isn't accelerating either. Weather across the Belt remains conducive to crop development with **no stress premium being priced yet**. The grain liquidation question from Monday might be answering itself through exhaustion rather than weather catalyst.
sections.catalyst: Sluggish demand continues but fund liquidation pace slowing
sections.bottom_line: Sideways drift suggests liquidation exhausting itself, not weather taking control yet.

sections.title: MILK DROPS ON PARLOR PRESSURE
sections.body: Class III milk tumbled 3.2% to $16.16, giving back yesterday's overnight gains as **parlor monitoring technology and mastitis detection systems hit adoption curves**. The drop reverses Tuesday's processing strength story, suggesting the milk complex is more volatile than directional right now. Block cheese and barrel spreads are widening again, indicating the processing margin squeeze that drove yesterday's strength was temporary. **Dairy remains the most volatile protein complex**, swinging between processing optimism and feed cost pressure week to week.
sections.catalyst: Parlor technology adoption and processing margin shifts
sections.bottom_line: Milk volatility continues, yesterday's gains erased on processing pressure.

outside_the_pit.title: MASCHIO GASPARDO opens first full North American store in Corn Belt
outside_the_pit.body: The Italian equipment manufacturer is betting on US farmer demand for specialty tillage and seeding equipment. Location in the heart of corn country signals confidence in domestic market growth.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA launches $60M processor action plan for small meat plants
outside_the_pit.body: New funding aims to reduce regulatory burdens and support very small processing operations. The move addresses concentration concerns but won't immediately change cattle pricing dynamics.

outside_the_pit.title: Equipment tariff reductions won't translate to farmer savings, economist says
outside_the_pit.body: Agricultural Economic Insights reports no evidence that equipment tariff cuts pass through to farm-level pricing. The finding suggests trade policy benefits flow to dealers and manufacturers, not producers.

spread_to_watch.label: August live cattle / August feeder ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Feeders dropping faster than live cattle creates an unusually wide ratio, pricing biological risk into the feeder complex specifically. **This spread should narrow if screwworm containment holds**, but stay wide if movement restrictions persist through summer grazing season.

basis.headline: Corn basis steady, cattle basis yelling in South Texas
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis holding firm** as ethanol demand stays consistent and river logistics work smoothly. **South Texas cattle basis fall sharply expected** as quarantine restrictions limit movement options and create artificial supply concentration. Western cattle basis staying defensive until screwworm containment story clarifies. Soybean basis soft but not deteriorating, matching the futures drift.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: Thursday's sideways action suggests liquidation exhausting itself through drift rather than drama. Grains are grinding toward resolution but weather still isn't providing a catalyst.

the_more_you_know.title: Why screwworm spreads faster than markets price
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.8% feeder drop on the Texas screwworm case looks measured, but the biology argues for more concern. **New World screwworm was eradicated from the US in 1982 after causing $750 million annually in cattle losses**. The female fly lays 200-300 eggs in wounds on warm-blooded animals, with larvae eating the host alive. A single generation cycle takes 21 days, meaning exponential spread without aggressive containment. Markets are pricing quarantine success, not biological reality.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales - soybeans under 300K MT keeps the chart in charge
Screwworm quarantine expansion - additional Texas counties would crater cattle basis
Weather forecast shift - heat stress models become relevant if temps spike

daily_quote.text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

source_summary: CME settlement prices, USDA screwworm confirmation, weekly export sales pending
